spec_match.rationale: The reference table and code snippets correctly describe how referrer-policy modifiers are configured in CSS url() functions.

interactive.rationale: The 'live request lab' exists, but it cheats by using JavaScript fetch() with the referrerPolicy option instead of testing the actual CSS URL request modifier.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Aligns with the parent index description of letting users test each policy value and observe its effect.

edge_cases.rationale: The page doesn't demonstrate cross-origin downgrade transitions or cross-origin vs same-origin variations with real resource requests.

references.rationale: Provides direct reference links to the ChromeStatus entry, CSS Values spec, and MDN Referrer-Policy guidelines.

design_system.rationale: Conforms perfectly to the layout, color rules, and styling of the core design system.

openQuestions.title: Refactor Request Lab to use CSS background-image and dynamic SVG echo
openQuestions.detail: The request lab currently uses standard JS fetch(), which completely bypasses the CSS referrer-policy() modifier under test. We should refactor the demo to dynamically update a background-image rule carrying the modifier (e.g. background-image: url('/v150/css-url-request-modifiers/referrer-echo-image.svg' referrer-policy(no-referrer))). The server route can generate a dynamic SVG image containing the received 'Referer' header string so the user sees the real-world result rendered directly inside a CSS background block.

openQuestions.title: Refactor the capability probe to target CSS URL modifiers
openQuestions.detail: The live capability probe section is currently copy-pasted from a generic template and tests unrelated APIs (e.g., navigator.gpu, scheduler). It should be rewritten to specifically check and report support for CSS URL request modifiers using CSS.supports('background-image', 'url("a.png" referrer-policy(no-referrer))').

summary: A useful explanation of referrer policies in CSS URL modifiers, but the interactive 'request lab' is fundamentally flawed because it relies on JS fetch() instead of native CSS background-image loading. Tying it to a real CSS-driven background request returning a dynamic SVG header echo would make this a premier, compliant demo. The capability probe is also generic and needs targeting.
